main_summary.summary: Dr. John Carlos, bronze medalist in the 200 meters at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics and co-author with Tommie Smith of the black-gloved salute on the victory stand, sat for this interview with David Cline of Virginia Tech in Brooklyn on August 18, 2013, for the Civil Rights History Project. Born June 5, 1945, on Lenox Avenue in Harlem to Earl Carlos Sr., a Camden, SC-born WWI veteran and shoemaker, and a Cuban-born mother, Carlos describes growing up between the Cotton Club and the Savoy Ballroom; meeting Fred Astaire as a hustling child performer; the heroin epidemic that disintegrated Harlem families; his 'Robin Hood' freight-train raids to feed neighbors; and being routed into competitive running by NYPD detectives Lester and Brown, who steered him to Joe Yancey at the New York Pioneer Club. He covers his schooling at Adam Clayton Powell Sr. High, Haaren High, and the School of Machine and Metal Trades; his hearing of Malcolm X at the 116th Street mosque; his disillusionment with East Texas State University under a racist coach; his 1968 meeting with Dr. King, Andrew Young, Dr. Harry Edwards, and Ken Noel about the Olympic Project for Human Rights; the planning of the Mexico City demonstration with Tommie Smith and Peter Norman; and the personal costs that followed, including his first wife's suicide.
key_themes: Harlem childhood and the heroin epidemic
Racism at East Texas State University
Influence of Malcolm X, Marcus Garvey, Paul Robeson, and Dr. King
main_summary.historical_significance: Carlos is one of only three living first-person witnesses to the most-televised single act of civil-rights-era athlete protest. His account here is the only Smithsonian/Library of Congress interview in which he supplies the full provenance of each symbol on the victory stand (black shirt, beads, sockless feet, the shared pair of gloves, Paul Hoffman's button) and names the specific Harvard rowers who supplied the OPHR pin for Peter Norman. He provides the only known first-person record of his Americana Hotel meeting with Dr. King, in which King committed to serve as second-in-command of the Olympic boycott under Dr. Harry Edwards after Carlos returned from Memphis.

chapters.title: Harlem Childhood and Family
chapters.summary: Carlos describes being born June 5, 1945, on Lenox Avenue to Earl Carlos Sr. (a Camden SC-born WWI veteran and Harlem shoemaker) and a Cuban-born mother who immigrated at 17. He grew up between the Cotton Club and the Savoy Ballroom, learned to hustle for silver dollars in front of the clubs, and was given lasting performance advice by Fred Astaire to 'give the people a good show for the money.'

chapters.title: Heroin, White Flight, and Robin Hood
chapters.summary: Carlos recounts the arrival of heroin in Harlem following the bootleg-liquor 'King Kong' era, the disintegration of Black family units as fathers became addicted, and the white flight he observed from a child's vantage point. He adopted Robin Hood as a personal model, distinguishing 'God's law' from 'man's law,' and began breaking into freight yards near Yankee Stadium to redistribute food and textiles to neighbors.

chapters.title: Swimming Dream and Pioneer Club
chapters.summary: Carlos describes hearing about the English Channel swim and the Olympic Games on the radio and announcing to his father that he would be the first Black American Olympic swimmer, only to be told that segregated pools and clubs barred his path. Detectives Lester and Brown of the 32nd Precinct at 135th Street confronted him about his freight-yard raids, told him he had a gift for running, and steered him to Joseph 'Joe' Yancey at the New York Pioneer Club at the 168th Street Armory.
related_events: Carlos's recruitment into the New York Pioneer Club

chapters.title: High School and the Building of a Legend
chapters.summary: Carlos describes wanting to attend a high school with a swim program, being routed instead to Adam Clayton Powell Sr. High and Haaren High, and finally transferring to the School of Machine and Metal Trades, where he ran on the track team and broke records at the 168th Street Armory. He recounts post-meet outdoor races against open-class winners that built his reputation throughout New York's PSAL.

chapters.title: Athletics as Platform and the Boycott Argument
chapters.summary: Carlos articulates his shift in viewing track and field as a springboard for political voice rather than personal acclaim. He compares the 1968 boycott debate to embargoes that harm only the population, explains why he no longer endorses boycotts, and uses an extended train-and-consensus metaphor to describe how those who avoided the burns of 1968 cannot retroactively claim the credit.
related_events: 1968 Olympic Project for Human Rights boycott debate

chapters.title: Religious and Political Influences
chapters.summary: Carlos describes his religious search leading him to Adam Clayton Powell Sr.'s Abyssinian Baptist Church and his shock at Adam Clayton Powell Jr.'s appearance preaching one Sunday. He recounts studying Marcus Garvey's Back-to-Africa movement, Paul Robeson's sacrifice of an entertainment career for political voice, and finally hearing Malcolm X at the 116th Street mosque, where he became Malcolm's walking companion until the Audubon Ballroom assassination.
related_events: Audubon Ballroom assassination of Malcolm X (1965)

chapters.title: Meeting Dr. King
chapters.summary: Carlos describes the meeting at the Americana Hotel with Dr. Harry Edwards, Ken Noel, Andrew Young, and Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. about the proposed 1968 Olympic boycott. He recounts King's lake-and-rock metaphor for the boycott's reach, King's commitment to serve as second-in-command under Dr. Edwards upon return from Memphis, and King's answer about why he would return despite the death threats: to stand for those who could not stand for themselves.
related_events: 1968 Americana Hotel meeting on the Olympic boycott

chapters.title: East Texas State University
chapters.summary: Carlos describes his recruitment to East Texas State University by Pete Peterson, his arrival to whites-only and colored restrooms at the Dallas-Fort Worth airport, and a coach who called him 'boy' and slurs in front of his wife and infant daughter. He recounts the racist coach attempting to assault him with a hammer after Carlos refused a pre-conference workout, white teammate Terry Barnett (whom Carlos places with John Brown and Peter Norman) talking him into staying long enough to win the Lone Star Conference championship, and a bar incident in Austin that crystallized his decision to leave.

chapters.title: The Mexico City Victory Stand
chapters.summary: Carlos describes the planning of the demonstration in the 25 minutes between the semifinal and the medal ceremony, his asking Peter Norman whether he believed in human rights, Paul Hoffman of the Harvard rowing crew throwing down the OPHR button for Norman, and the symbols carried onto the podium: black shirt covering the USA jersey, Tommie Smith's black scarf, beads for Black lynching victims and those thrown overboard in the Middle Passage, sockless feet for poverty, and the gloves themselves. He describes a childhood vision on Lenox Avenue of an arena where joy turned to venom at his raised hand.

chapters.title: Aftermath and Personal Cost
chapters.summary: Carlos recounts the New York papers turning him from 'great' to 'neighborhood bum' within thirty days, his father in the hospital weeping at the headlines until Carlos asked him to trust the son he knew over the paper. He describes the silence of fellow Olympians who told him he had ruined his life, his first wife's suicide, his children's school suffering, years without Christmas presents, and the unsolicited grocery delivery from Ron Freeman that he never forgot. He frames George Foreman not as antagonist but as longtime supporter against right-wing-press distortion.
related_events: Post-1968 retaliation against Carlos and his family

chapters.title: Teaching and Legacy
chapters.summary: Carlos describes his work as a high school teacher and his approach of melting down to a student's level rather than standing above them. He recounts being told the previous evening at New York's first PSAL track alumni reunion of the long-term effects of his one-on-one mentoring on the runners he coached, the 80-foot mural of him in Sydney, and the statues at San Jose State, with the empty slot where Peter Norman's likeness would have stood at Norman's own request.

loc_video.caption: John Carlos oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in New York, New York, 2013 August 18
